did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Z-score-based local color correction method and a privacy-preserving segmentation approach (utilizing POB number system and image permutation) to perform skin lesion segmentation on dermoscopic images from the ISIC and PH2 datasets. It compares its results against traditional clustering algorithms like k-Means, Otsu's method, and Fuzzy C-Means, which are computational image processing techniques used for segmentation in dermatology.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a 'Z-score-based secure biomedical model' for skin lesion segmentation. It details a specific methodology involving privacy-preserving techniques (POB number system, image permutation) and local color correction. The authors conduct experiments on public datasets (ISIC 2016, ISIC 2017, PH2), compare their proposed method against existing schemes (k-Means, Otsu's, Fuzzy C-Means) and challenge winners, and report quantitative results using metrics like Dice Coefficient, Jaccard Index, Sensitivity, Specificity, and Accuracy in Tables 1-7. This structure indicates an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Z -Score-Based Secure Biomedical Model for Effective Skin Lesion Segmentation Over eHealth Cloud
journal: ACM Trans. Multimedia Comput. Commun. Appl.
brief_summary: This study proposes a privacy-preserving Z-score-based secure biomedical model for effective skin lesion segmentation over eHealth cloud platforms to address data privacy concerns in Melanoma detection. The method utilizes Z-score-based local color correction to resolve pixel ambiguity and a Partially Ordered Binary (POB) number system with image permutation to ensure security without intermediate decryption. Experiments were conducted on the ISIC 2016, ISIC 2017, and PH2 dermoscopic image datasets, comparing the approach against k-Means, Otsu’s, and Fuzzy C-Means clustering algorithms. The proposed method achieved superior segmentation quality in the encrypted domain, with Dice Coefficient and Jaccard Index scores significantly higher than existing schemes. Furthermore, the results were found to be very close to those of the winners of the ISBI 2016 and 2017 challenges, demonstrating that high accuracy can be maintained while preserving patient data privacy.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on skin lesion segmentation for Melanoma detection, specifically using datasets from the 'Skin Lesion Analysis toward Melanoma Detection' challenge (ISBI 2016 and 2017). The segmentation task targets the lesion area within dermoscopic images, which are primarily associated with melanoma in the context of these challenges.

The study performs segmentation on 'skin lesions' generally, treating the lesion as the foreground to be segmented from the background skin. The datasets (ISIC 2016, ISIC 2017, PH2) contain various types of skin lesions, but the paper does not explicitly list or distinguish specific benign or malignant subtypes (e.g., nevus, BCC) beyond the general 'Melanoma' context of the challenge.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion area in dermoscopic images
all_task_types.details: The paper performs skin lesion segmentation, clustering similar pixels to differentiate the lesion (foreground) from the background skin. It uses metrics like Dice Coefficient, Jaccard Index, Sensitivity, Specificity, and Accuracy to evaluate the quality of the segmented masks against ground truth annotations.

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2016 Dataset
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic images from the 'Skin Lesion Analysis toward Melanoma Detection' challenge at the International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI 2016), provided by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC).

use.training: Used for training and evaluation of the proposed segmentation method in the encrypted domain.
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluation of the proposed segmentation method in the encrypted domain.
use.other: Results compared against winners of the ISBI 2016 challenge.

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2017 Dataset
datasets_used.description: An extension of the ISBI 2016 images, contributing a total of 2000 and 600 training and testing images, known as ISBI 2017.

use.training: Used for training and evaluation of the proposed segmentation method in the encrypted domain.
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluation of the proposed segmentation method in the encrypted domain.
use.other: Results compared against winners of the ISBI 2017 challenge.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset consisting of 200 dermoscopic images acquired at the Dermatology Service of Hospital Pedro Hispano, Matosinhos, Portugal, provided by Mendonca et al.

use.testing: Used for evaluation of the proposed segmentation method in the encrypted domain.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate segmentation quality by measuring the similarity between the predicted segmentation mask and the ground truth.
To evaluate segmentation quality by measuring the intersection over union of the predicted segmentation mask and the ground truth.
To evaluate the proportion of actual positive pixels (lesion) correctly identified by the segmentation model.
To evaluate the proportion of actual negative pixels (background) correctly identified by the segmentation model.
To evaluate the overall proportion of correctly classified pixels (both lesion and background) in the segmentation results.
Used for local color correction to differentiate image pixels from ambiguity by modifying intensity values based on mean and standard deviation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared the proposed method against existing schemes and challenge winners using descriptive statistics (Dice Coefficient, Jaccard Index, Sensitivity, Specificity, Accuracy) and computational time, noting 'significant results' and 'negligible difference' in scores without statistical significance testing.

training_images.metric: Dice Coefficient (DC), Jaccard Index (JI), Sensitivity (SE), Specificity (SP), Accuracy (AC)
training_images.notes: The proposed method processes images in the encrypted domain (ED), while existing schemes process in the plain domain (PD).

testing_images.metric: Dice Coefficient (DC), Jaccard Index (JI), Sensitivity (SE), Specificity (SP), Accuracy (AC)
testing_images.notes: The proposed approach achieved results very close to the challenge winners despite processing in the encrypted domain. The average difference between the proposed method and the winners was: DC 0.09, JI 0.11, SE 0.12, SP 0.04, AC 0.11.

training_images.metric: Dice Coefficient (DC), Jaccard Index (JI), Sensitivity (SE), Specificity (SP), Accuracy (AC)

testing_images.metric: Dice Coefficient (DC), Jaccard Index (JI), Sensitivity (SE), Specificity (SP), Accuracy (AC)
testing_images.notes: The proposed approach achieved results very close to the challenge winners. The average difference between the proposed method and the winners was: DC 0.07, JI 0.09, SE 0.06, SP 0.06, AC 0.09.

PH2 Dataset.metric: Dice Coefficient (DC), Jaccard Index (JI), Sensitivity (SE), Specificity (SP), Accuracy (AC)
PH2 Dataset.notes: The proposed method showed superior segmentation quality compared to existing schemes on the PH2 dataset.

architecture_used.main_model: Z-score-based secure biomedical model (privacy-preserving segmentation approach)

architecture_details.methodology: Non-learning based algorithmic approach utilizing Z-score-based local color correction for pixel differentiation and Partially Ordered Binary (POB) number system with image permutation for privacy preservation.
preprocessing_steps: Image permutation using secret key k1
Pixel modification using POB number system with parameters n and t
Normalization of pixel intensity values to [0, 255]

training_details.details: The proposed approach does not require explicit training datasets or model learning; it processes single images directly.

classification_or_output_head.details: Segmentation is performed by clustering similar pixels in the encrypted domain using k-Means.

preprocessing_techniques: Center cropping (CenCrop) to remove unnecessary image regions
Disk filtering for hair removal to eliminate unwanted hair streaks
Z-score-based local color correction to differentiate pixel values near the mean and reduce ambiguity
Normalization of pixel intensity values to [0, 255]
conclusions: The proposed Z-score-based secure biomedical model effectively performs skin lesion segmentation on dermoscopic images while preserving patient data privacy by processing images in the encrypted domain without intermediate decryption.

The method utilizes Z-score-based local color correction to resolve pixel ambiguity and a Partially Ordered Binary (POB) number system with image permutation to ensure security against third-party cloud servers.
The proposed approach achieved superior segmentation quality compared to existing clustering schemes (k-Means, Otsu’s method, and Fuzzy C-Means) across the ISBI 2016, ISBI 2017, and PH2 datasets.

On the ISBI 2016 testing dataset, the proposed method achieved a Dice Coefficient of 0.80 and Jaccard Index of 0.71, which are very close to the winners of the ISBI 2016 challenge (DC: 0.88-0.91, JI: 0.81-0.84), with an average difference of 0.09 for DC and 0.11 for JI.

On the ISBI 2017 testing dataset, the proposed method achieved a Dice Coefficient of 0.77 and Jaccard Index of 0.66, showing negligible difference compared to the winners of the ISBI 2017 challenge (DC: 0.84-0.85, JI: 0.73-0.74), with an average difference of 0.07 for DC and 0.09 for JI.
On the PH2 dataset, the proposed method achieved a Dice Coefficient of 0.88 and Jaccard Index of 0.79, demonstrating superior performance over existing schemes.

The study concludes that high segmentation accuracy can be maintained while preserving patient data privacy, with the performance degradation being negligible compared to state-of-the-art methods that process images in the plain domain.
The security of the proposed approach was analyzed using a challenge-response game model, confirming its effectiveness in protecting medical data on eHealth cloud platforms.
important_remarks: The proposed method is a non-learning, algorithmic approach (Z-score preprocessing + k-Means clustering) rather than a deep learning model, which limits its ability to capture complex semantic features compared to state-of-the-art CNNs.

The method processes images in an encrypted domain (ED) to preserve privacy, but this results in performance degradation compared to the ISBI challenge winners who processed images in the plain domain (PD); the average Dice Coefficient difference was 0.09 for ISBI 2016 and 0.07 for ISBI 2017.

The study relies on public datasets (ISBI 2016, ISBI 2017, PH2) without external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting the assessment of generalizability to diverse skin types and lesion presentations.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the superiority of the proposed method over existing schemes; comparisons are based on descriptive statistics of mean scores.

The segmentation performance on the ISBI 2017 testing set (Dice Coefficient 0.77) is notably lower than on the ISBI 2016 testing set (Dice Coefficient 0.80) and the PH2 dataset (Dice Coefficient 0.88), suggesting potential dataset-specific bias or variability in segmentation difficulty.

The paper does not report sensitivity to hyperparameters (e.g., POB number system parameters n and t, or secret keys) or computational complexity analysis beyond average time, which are critical for deployment in real-time eHealth cloud environments.
The security analysis relies on a theoretical challenge-response game model rather than empirical robustness testing against specific cryptographic attacks or side-channel analysis.
